[Home](../)

---
title: "HW 6"
format:
  html:
    output-file: "index"
embed-resources: true
---

**Exercise 1. 
a) What is the most interesting lesson, guide, or piece of advice Tufte offers you in this chapter?**

In the Reading, Tufte emphasizes on how graphics can display what numbers alone cannot. It gives a clear story and a purpose with the graphics. From John Snow's cholera map to Marey's train schedule, It answers questions and revealing something new.

Another example is Anscombe's Quartet; four datasets with same statistical summary looks entirely different when visualized.

**b)**
![Marey's Train Schedule](../hw6/data304 ss.png)

I chose Marey's Train schedule. I thought this was very interesting visualization. At a first glance, it just looks like a grid with a bunch of scribbles of lines. However looking deep, it conveys a lot of information.

Encoding channels:

x-axis = Time of day
y-axis = Geographic location, spaced proportionally to actual distance
Slope of the line = Speed of the train
Horizontal segments = How long the train stops at a station
Intersection of two lines = The exact time and place where two trains traveling in opposite directions pass each other

The y axis segments setting it proportionally will be very hard for me to do it manually.

Tufte's point: Every visual elemten has a purpose, and nothing is decorative. "complex ideas communicated with clarity, precision, and efficiency"

**Exercise 2**
I learned the importance of iterating through different visualizations with the same data. Each types of visualization reveals somethings different. 

It was surprising to learn that pie charts are not an effective visualization type. Since they are so widely used in news and articles, I had always assumed they were a reliable way to present data. However, Knaflic explains that our eyes struggle to accurately compare areas, making it difficult to tell which segment is bigger when the sizes are similar.

**Exercise 3**
STEP 1. 
-Bar charts should start at zero baseline. This chart starts at 50%
-Too many data labels and they look cluttered.
-Two different visualization type is combined (line & bar chart) which is hard to interpret what the visualization is trying to show.

STEP 2.
-Start y axis baseline at 0%
-minimize the data labels, leaving out the only important features
-use one visualization type only to present important information

STEP 3.

```{r}
library(vegawidget)
graph <- list(
  `$schema` = "https://vega.github.io/schema/vega-lite/v6.json",
  width = 700,
  height= 200,
  data = list(values = list(
    list(Date = "Q1-2017", `Completion Rate` = 0.91, `Response Rate` = 0.023),
    list(Date = "Q2-2017", `Completion Rate` = 0.93, `Response Rate` = 0.018),
    list(Date = "Q3-2017", `Completion Rate` = 0.91, `Response Rate` = 0.028),
    list(Date = "Q4-2017", `Completion Rate` = 0.89, `Response Rate` = 0.023),
    list(Date = "Q1-2018", `Completion Rate` = 0.84, `Response Rate` = 0.034),
    list(Date = "Q2-2018", `Completion Rate` = 0.88, `Response Rate` = 0.027),
    list(Date = "Q3-2018", `Completion Rate` = 0.91, `Response Rate` = 0.026),
    list(Date = "Q4-2018", `Completion Rate` = 0.87, `Response Rate` = 0.039),
    list(Date = "Q1-2019", `Completion Rate` = 0.83, `Response Rate` = 0.028)
  )),
  vconcat = list(
    list(
      title = "Completion Rate Over Time",
      mark = "line",
      encoding = list(
        x = list(field = "Date", type = "ordinal"),
        y = list(field = "Completion Rate", type = "quantitative")
      )
    ),
    list(
      title = "Response Rate Over Time",
      mark = "line",
      encoding = list(
        x = list(field = "Date", type = "ordinal"),
        y = list(field = "Response Rate", type = "quantitative")
      )
    )
  )
)
as_vegaspec(graph)
```
